coredata.dc:title: Chemically anchored black phosphorus/chromophore composite luminescent materials towards solid-state photoluminescence and electroluminescence

coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yun
Zhang, Junyu
Xu, Huilong
Wang, Xijie
Zhao, Bolin
Zhang, Baohua
Ni, Zhonghai
Li, Guoling
Zhang, Yuwei
coredata.dc:description: As a promising two-dimensional (2D) material, black phosphorus (BP) has gained great attention because of its intrinsic physicochemical characteristics. However, BP is easy to be oxidized and form various surface defects, which decreases the charge transport and makes luminescence difficult. Herein, we report a facile strategy to synthesize chemically anchored BP/chromophore composite luminescent materials by integrating 2D BP nanosheets with boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes through simple sonication reactions. Solids of BP composite materials are brightly fluorescent with 5 orders of magnitude increase of photoluminescence intensity compared with the pure solids of BP and BODIPY. 2D BP nanosheets with large specific surface area and lone pair electrons facilitate chemisorption of BODIPY by forming covalent interactions between P and B/N atoms, which not only passivates the surface of BP and eliminates the oxidation, but also prevents the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) issue of BODIPY. Furtherly, the organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) based on the BP/chromophore composite luminescent materials were first successfully achieved, the electroluminescence spectra of which cover the full spectrum of red, green, and blue visible light. This work provides a brand-new approach to further design and development of high mobility and high luminous efficiency composite luminescent materials and devices based on BP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-dimensional luminescent material
Black phosphorus
Solid-state emission
Photoluminescence
Electroluminescence

OLEDs devices fabrication and performance evaluation
Synthesis of BP/BODIPY composite luminescent materials
The interaction mechanism between BP and BODIPY

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of G-BODIPY, R-BODIPY, and B-BODIPY.
PL spectra of solutions and solids of R-BODIPY(a), G-BODIPY(b), and B-BODIPY(c). The photographs of solutions and solid films under 365 nm UV light are shown in the insert.
Synthesis procedures of BP/BODIPY composite luminescent materials and mechanism of solid-state emission.

(a) PL spectra of solid films with different mass ratios of BP nanosheets and G-BODIPY. (b) PL spectra of the solid-state of R/G/B-BP/BODIPY composite luminescent materials. (c) The photographs of BP nanosheets and solid films of R/G/B-BP/BODIPY under 365 nm UV light.
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and the energy dispersive spectroscopy elemental maps of G-BP/BODIPY.
(a) TEM images of G-BP/BODIPY, scale bar: 200 nm. (b) The blowup of the area indicated by the yellow square in (a). (c) LSCM image of G-BP/BODIPY, λex = 450–490 nm, λem = 515 nm, scale bar: 2 μm.
(a) Raman scattering of G-BP/BODIPY and BP nanosheets; (b) FT-IR spectra of G-BODIPY, BP nanosheets, and G-BP/BODIPY.
XPS spectra of BP nanosheets and G-BP/BODIPY: (a) P 2p core-level, (b) N 1s core-level, and (c) B 1s core-level.
(a) Device architecture diagram, (b) Normalized EL spectra, (c) CIE color coordinates, (d) J−V-L characteristics of these OLEDs using R/G/B-BP/BODIPY as the EML.

head.ce:title: Chemically anchored black phosphorus/chromophore composite luminescent materials towards solid-state photoluminescence and electroluminescence

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a promising two-dimensional (2D) material, black phosphorus (BP) has gained great attention because of its intrinsic physicochemical characteristics. However, BP is easy to be oxidized and form various surface defects, which decreases the charge transport and makes luminescence difficult. Herein, we report a facile strategy to synthesize chemically anchored BP/chromophore composite luminescent materials by integrating 2D BP nanosheets with boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes through simple sonication reactions. Solids of BP composite materials are brightly fluorescent with 5 orders of magnitude increase of photoluminescence intensity compared with the pure solids of BP and BODIPY. 2D BP nanosheets with large specific surface area and lone pair electrons facilitate chemisorption of BODIPY by forming covalent interactions between P and B/N atoms, which not only passivates the surface of BP and eliminates the oxidation, but also prevents the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) issue of BODIPY. Furtherly, the organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) based on the BP/chromophore composite luminescent materials were first successfully achieved, the electroluminescence spectra of which cover the full spectrum of red, green, and blue visible light. This work provides a brand-new approach to further design and development of high mobility and high luminous efficiency composite luminescent materials and devices based on BP.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Black phosphorus (BP), a two-dimensional (2D) material, has aroused tremendous interest, because of its distinct structure and physicochemical properties including large specific surface area, anisotropic properties in plane, good mechanical strength, tunable direct band gap and high charge carrier mobility [1–5]. The advantages of BP lead to potential application in transistors, photoelectric devices, photodetectors, biomedicine, energy conversion and many other fields. For example, single-layered BP exhibits high mobility of 1000 cm2V−1s−1, which has been employed already in high-mobility field-effect transistors [6,7]. BP also represents as charge transport materials for many other applications such as organic solar cells [8,9], perovskite solar cells [10,11]. However, BP is very easy to be oxidized and form various complex chemical and physical surface defects [12,13]. They not only affect the charge transport properties negatively but also make effective exciton recombination luminescence difficult [14,15], since the defect-related nonradiative centers easily convert the photon energy to heat energy in bulk BP. Up to now, it is still impossible to obtain effective luminescent materials based on 2D layered materials of BP.

Recently, the fluorescent BP quantum dots (QDs) in solution have been reported, the excitons are successfully bound to the top of BP quantum dot to achieve photoluminescence (PL) in the solution state [16–18]. However, due to the restriction of quantum size limiting effect, the excellent large conjugation and high mobility characteristics of BP will be affected [19]. Up to now, although self-luminous BP QDs have high fluorescence quantum efficiency, their application is still limited to the field of PL in solution. There are no reports of BP displaying solid-state PL or electroluminescent (EL) devices using BP as the luminescent layer.

BP has the unique molecular structure. each P atom of BP exposes a pair of lone pair electrons which can be covalent modified through various chemical reactions [20–22]. Several approaches have been reported to functionalize BP through covalent or/and non-covalent interactions. For example, successful modification using C60 [23], boronic derivatives of pyrene [24], and conjugated polymers [25] have been demonstrated. In addition, we also reported a covalent modification of BP by a chemical reaction with sodium alkoxide to decrease the degradation because of the encapsulation [26]. As a result, 2D BP nanosheets with the large specific surface area and lone pair electrons can be integrated with planar and conjugated organic molecules by chemisorption. The family of boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes are known for their high absorptivity and fluorescent quantum yields, long singlet excited state lifetimes, and tunable emissions [27–30]. However, the tight intermolecular π-π stacking between conjugate planes of BODIPY molecules leads to aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effects, which prevent their use as solid-state fluorescent materials [31–33]. While the molecule of BODIPY containing B and N atoms is expected to show high chemical reactivity to form covalent bonds with BP, which can prevent π-π stacking interactions between the conjugate planes of BODIPY.

Herein, we propose a new strategy to synthesize chemically anchored BP/chromophore composite luminescent materials by simply sonicating BP nanosheets and BODIPY in acetonitrile solution. These fluorescent dyes were successfully chemically bonded to the BP, which not only passivated the surface of BP and eliminated the oxidation, but also prevented the ACQ issue of the fluorescent dyes. Solids of BP/BODIPY composite materials are brightly fluorescent with 5 orders of magnitude increase of PL intensity compared with the pure solids of BP and BODIPY. Furtherly, we first successfully constructed a solid state EL device based on the chemically anchored BP/chromophore composite luminescent materials. The EL spectra of the model devices cover the full spectrum of red, green, and blue visible light. This work opens the door for the further design and development of BP-based composite light-emitting materials and devices with high mobility and high luminous efficiency, and is expected to promote the further development of this field.

Except for R/G/B-BODIPY, all of the chemicals and reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China) and used as received without purifying further. All chemicals used were of analytical grade. The BP used here was purchased from Zhongke Experimental Materials (China) and preserved in an inert atmosphere.
The red, green, and blue BODIPY dyes (R-BODIPY, G-BODIPY, and B-BODIPY) were synthesized separately according to the literature [34–36] and the synthetic pathway of which was shown in Fig. 1 .

2,4-dimethylpyrrole (2.1 g, 22 mmol) and 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde (10 mmol, 1.1 g) were dissolved in 200 mL dichloromethane (CH2Cl2). Then, one drop of trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) was added and the solution was stirred at room temperature for 5 h, followed by adding dropwise a CH2Cl2 solution (50 mL) of 2,3-dichloro-5,6-dicyano-p-benzoquinone (DDQ) (6 mmol, 1.36 g). After stirring for 12 h, 8 mL of triethylamine (Et3N) and 10 mL of boron trifluoride etherate (BF3OEt2) were added rapidly. The reaction solution was stirred for 5 h, and then washed three times with water. After dried by Na2SO4, the reaction solution was evaporated to remove the solvent, and the residue was purified by silica gel column chromatography with an eluent of 1:10 (v/v) CH2Cl2/hexane, affording 2.13 g of compound G-BODIPY as a red solid (yield 63 %). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ 9.86 (s, 1H), 7.12–7.09 (m, 2H), 6.93–6.90 (m, 2H), 6.16 (s, 2H), 2.44 (d, J = 6.5 Hz, 8H), 1.42 (d, J = 6.5 Hz, 7H). MALDI TOF-MS: m/z 340.1559 [M]+ 340.1507.

A mixture of G-BODIPY (0.13 g, 0.4 mmol), 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde (0.22 g, 2.0 mmol), piperidine (0.96 mL), dissolved in 30 mL dry toluene were heated for 20 h under reflux, using a Dean-Stark condenser. After dried by Na2SO4, the reaction solution was evaporated to remove the solvent, and the residue was purified by silica gel column chromatography with an eluent of 10:1 (v/v) CH2Cl2/methanol, affording 0.162 g of compound R-BODIPY as a blue solid (yield 76 %). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ79.98 (s, 1H), 9.84 (s, 1H), 7.66–7.43 (m, 3H), 7.33 (d, J = 16.3 Hz, 1H), 7.17 (d, J = 7.9 Hz, 1H), 7.05–6.60 (m, 4H), 1.48 (s, 3H). MALDI TOF-MS: m/z 548.2083 [M]+ 548.2013.

BF3OEt2 (2.85 g, 20 mmol) was slowly added at room temperature to a stirred dichloroethane solution of dipyrid-2-ylamine (4.25 g, 25 mmol). The reaction mixture was heated at 80 °C for 3 h, cooled to room temperature, diluted with CH2Cl2 (100 mL), and washed with water (50 mL). After dried by Na2SO4, the reaction solution was evaporated to remove the solvent, and the residue was purified by silica gel column chromatography with an eluent of 1:5 (v/v) CH2Cl2/hexane, affording 2.16 g of compound B-BODIPY as a yellow solid (yield 40 %). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ8.40 (dd, J = 5.5, 3.3 Hz, 1H), 8.10 (ddt, J = 10.1, 4.8, 2.4 Hz, 1H), 7.30 (td, J = 10.2, 9.4, 3.3 Hz, 2H). MALDI TOF-MS: m/z 219.0779 [M]+ 219.0726.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of R/G/B– BP/BODIPY composite materials
ce:section.ce:para: 8 mg of bulk BP was added to 14 mL of acetonitrile and sonicated for 14 h in the glovebox. After sonication, the original dispersion was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 15 min to remove the precipitate (2.4 mg) to yield a brown suspension of BP nanosheets. Then, 8 mg of R/G/B-BODIPY was added to the suspension to keep the actual mass ratio of BP:R/G/B-BODIPY as 7:10, followed by another 4 h sonication in the glovebox to receive the R/G/B-BP/BODIPY composite materials solution. Finally, the solid films of R/G/B-BP/BODIPY were obtained by depositing the solution on glass substrates.

The Edinburgh fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000) was used for characterizing the photophysical properties of those samples, in which steady-state PL measurements were triggered by an Xe2 xenon lamp, and a couped integration sphere was applied to determine the absolute PL quantum efficiency (PLQY). UV–Vis absorption spectra were characterized by a Cary 5000 UV–Vis–NIR spectrometer at room temperature. FTIR spectra were collected by using a Nicolet IS 5 infrared spectrometer. Raman spectra was conducted on a Horiba LabRam HR800 High-resolution confocal raman microscope with a 532 nm laser as the excitation light source. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was performed on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer with Al Kα radiation (1468.6 eV photons, 150 W). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were measured by a Zeiss Gemini Ultra-55 analytical field emission scanning electron microscope operated at 30 kV. Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) images were recorded on a Leica TCS SP8 X laser confocal microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were gained on a Tecnai G2 F20 analytical field emission scanning transmission electron microscope operated at 30 kV. Mass spectra were obtained on a Bruker Ultra-fleXtreme MALDI-TOF/TOF mass spectrometer.1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance III HD 600 MHz spectrometer and chemical shifts were referenced to tetramethylsilane (TMS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: OLEDs devices fabrication and performance evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: For the fabrications of organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) devices, ITO-coated glass substrates (ITO thickness = 110 nm, 15 Ω sq−1) were routinely cleaned by ultrasonic treatment, drying at 393 K and then UV-ozone treatment. Subsequently, the 50 nm thickness of poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly (styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) layer was deposited by spin-coating its water dispersion (Clevios PVP AI 4083, Heraeus) atop the ITO substrate and then dried at 393 K in air. After that, the poly(N-vinyl carbazole) (PVK) solution was spin-coated on top of the ITO/PEDOT: PSS layer in high purity nitrogen-filled glovebox, which was followed by drying at 373 K on the hotplate. The R/G/B-BP/BODIPY composite material solution was then spin-coated on top of the ITO/PEDOT: PSS/PVK layer, followed by drying at 333 K on the hotplate. Subsequently, the device samples were transferred into the thermal evaporator (EVOVAC, Angstrom Engineering Corp, Canada) and then thermally evaporated the layers of bis[2-(diphenylphosphino)phenyl]ether oxide (DPEPO) (10 nm), 1,3,5-tri(m-pyrid-3-yl-phenyl)-benzene (TmPyPB) (50 nm), LiF (1 nm) and Al (100 nm) in sequence. The pixel area of each OLED was 3.5 × 4 mm2, which is determined by the overlapping region between the ITO bottom anode and the Al top cathode.

The characterizations of those OLEDs were performed by using a commercial OLEDs testing system, i.e. FS-2000TR (FStar, Suzhou, China) coupled with a voltage-current source meter Keithley 2400 and a spectroradiometer CS2000A (Konica Mionlta). All these tests were conducted on the front side of these OLEDs, which were not encapsulated and measured under the ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BP/BODIPY composite luminescent materials
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the red, green, and blue BODIPY dyes (R-BODIPY, G-BODIPY, and B-BODIPY) were synthesized, the structures of which were characterized by the Mass spectra and 1H NMR spectra. The synthetic pathway is shown in Fig. 1. The synthetic procedures and the detailed data are shown in the Experiment section. The three BODIPY materials in the dichloromethane solutions showed strong PL emission (Fig. 2 a–c) with peaks at 672 nm, 515 nm, and 435 nm, respectively. However, the PL was significantly quenched when the BODIPY solutions were deposited on glass substrates to form the solid films, just as shown in the insert of Fig. 2a–c. The reason that solid-state R/G/B-BODIPY performed with little emission is the ACQ effects due to the tight stacking between conjugate planes of R/G/B-BODIPY molecules, as shown in Fig. 3 .

It was reported that BP can be covalent modified through various chemical reactions because of the lone electron pair at each P atom [32,33]. As shown in Fig. 3, R/G/B-BP/BODIPY composite materials were synthesized by the sonicating reaction between BP nanosheets and R/G/B-BODIPY in acetonitrile solution. The synthetic procedures and the detailed data are shown in the Experiment section. The solid films of R/G/B– BP/BODIPY were obtained by depositing the solution on glass substrates.

BP nanosheets were received by the same sonication method from bulk BP, the synthetic procedures of which were shown in the Experiment section. Fig. S2 displays the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the 2D layered structure of BP nanosheets. Experiments with different mass ratios of BP nanosheets and G-BODIPY (3:10, 7:10, and 14:10) were also performed, and the PL spectra of corresponding solid films compared with those of the BP nanosheets and G-BODIPY were shown in Fig. 4 a. It is worth mentioning that BP nanosheets had no PL emission in solid state (Fig. 4a–c). Solids of G-BP/BODIPY were brightly fluorescent with 5 orders of magnitude increase of fluorescence intensity compared with the pure solids of BODIPY and BP (Fig. 4a). With the increase of the scale of BP nanosheets, the solid-state fluorescence intensity of G-BP/BODIPY enhanced, which demonstrated that the incorporation of BP and BODIPY prevented the ACQ issue of BODIPY. But excessive BP will cause the lower relative load of the luminescent dyes, decreasing the fluorescence intensity. The optimal mass ratio of BP:G-BODIPY was settled as 7:10 according to the fluorescence intensity, which was applied in the B-BP/BODIPY and R–BP/BODIPY luminescent materials.

Fig. 4b and c shows the solid-state PL emission spectra of three R/G/B-BP/BODIPY composite luminescent materials and the photographs of the solid films under 365 nm UV light. Three primary colors of red, green, and blue solid-state fluorescent emission have been realized, which not only prevent the ACQ issue of BODIPY but also make it possible to tune the color emission of BP composite materials. Furthermore, PL emission spectra of solid state 2D BP/BODIPY luminescent materials possessed almost the same spectral shape as the corresponding BODIPY solutions (Fig. S1), which indicated the successful BP affection on BODIPY.

The morphology of R/G/B-BP/BODIPY composite materials was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. 5 and Fig. S3), which indicated that the composite materials remain the 2D layered structure of BP. The elemental mapping of these three materials clearly indicated the presence of C, P, N, F, and B, further confirming the incorporation of BP nanosheets and BODIPY.

Since the structure of three R/G/B-BODIPY were similar, the following analysis took G-BP/BODIPY as an example. Fig. 6 a, b displays the TEM images of G-BP/BODIPY composite materials in different scan areas, which also shows the few-layered nanoflakes with a diameter of around 200 nm. As shown in the TEM images, the clusters of G-BODIPY were dispersed on the surface of the BP nanosheets, which can also be proved by the elemental map. Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) image of G-BP/BODIPY composite materials (Fig. 6c) also revealed a brightly greenish fluorescence with the 2D layered structure, which was related to the SEM and TEM images. It is the 2D layered structure of BP that prevents the tight stacking between conjugate planes of BODIPY molecules and induces the solid-state emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction mechanism between BP and BODIPY
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical nature of the G-BP/BODIPY composites was studied by a series of spectroscopes. The Raman spectra (Fig. 7 a) analysis indicated the structural changes of BP. The spectrum of BP nanosheets exhibited three typical Raman peaks at 360.5, 435.5, and 462.0 cm−1, corresponding to the characteristic A1g, B2g, and A2g modes of BP, respectively [37]. Different from the control BP, all three peaks of the G-BP/BODIPY composites were red-shifted by around 6.5 cm−1, suggesting the chemical modification of the 2D BP lattice with the G-BODIPY, which inhibited the oscillation of P atoms and reduced the corresponding Raman scattering energy.

In addition, the covalent attachment of G-BODIPY onto the surface of BP was confirmed by the X-ray photoemission spectroscopic (XPS) measurements. The high-resolution P 2p, N 1s and B 1s XPS spectra of BP nanosheets and G-BP/BODIPY composites are compared in Fig. 8 . The two peaks at 129.34 and 130.14 eV of the G-BP/BODIPY in the P 2p XPS spectrum, which were correspond to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 states of P element [38], were slightly shifted to the lower energy region when compared to these of BP nanosheets. In the N 1s spectrum, a new peak at 399.5 eV corresponding to the N–P bond of BP/BODIPY was found compared with the BP nanosheets, which demonstrated the chemical attachment of BODIPY molecules on the BP [39]. Also, the formation of B–P bonds was observed in the B 1s XPS spectrum at 187.8 eV, further confirming that the BODIPY is chemically bound to the BP surface [40]. The formations of the N–P and B–P bonds were also observed in the IR spectrum of G-BP/BODIPY (Fig. 7b), which showed the new absorption band at 657 and 559 cm−1 separately compared with the pure G-BODIPY [41,42]. As a result, we believed that BODIPY was covalently bonded on the BP surface through the N–P and B–P bonds due to the lone electron pair at the P atom, preventing the π-stacking between the BODIPY molecules.

The interaction between BP and BODIPY can also be proved by the reduced oxidation degree of the BP/BODIPY composites. The lone electron pair of the P atom can be passivated by the BODIPY, which induces the elimination of surface oxidation of BP. As can be seen in Fig. 8a, the BP nanosheets showed an oxidized phosphorus (PxOy) peak at 133.6 eV belonging to partial oxidation [38]. However, the intensity of the P–O feature in G-BP/BODIPY was much weaker than the BP nanosheets, which confirmed the attachment of BODIPY on the surface preventing BP from oxidation reaction during the preparation and testing process. Furtherly, when exposed to air environments for 14 days, the integral area of control BP nanosheets approved a 28.7 % increase from 13.9 % to 42.6 % at 133.6 eV (Fig. S4). Nevertheless, the G-BP/BODIPY composites only received a 5.8 % increase, which indicated that an enhancement of the ambient stability can be achieved after modification.

Inspired by the achievements of much enhanced solid-state PL emission of BP/BODIPY and the fascinating electronic properties of BP, the EL properties of three R/G/B-BP/BODIPY luminescent materials were further evaluated as the emissive layer (EML) of OLEDs [43] (see Experimental sections for the preparing details). The OLEDs were fabricated in the configurations of ITO (110 nm)/PEDOT: PSS (50 nm)/PVK (10 nm)/(R/G/B-BP/BODIPY) emitters (10 nm)/DPEPO (10 nm)/TmPyPB (50 nm)/LiF (1 nm)/Al (100 nm), which were shown in Fig. 9 a. Herein, ITO was served as the transparent anode. PEDOT: PSS and PVK were selected as the hole injection layer (HIL) and hole transporting layer (HTL), respectively. DPEPO and TmPyPB served as the exciton-blocking layer (EBL) and electron transporting layer (ETL), respectively. Lithium fluoride (LiF) and Al were used as the electron-injecting layer (EIL) and cathode, respectively. The EL spectra, Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates, and current density−voltage−luminance (J−V−L) characteristics of fabricated OLEDs were shown in Fig. 9b–d and the key device data were summarized in Table S1.

As depicted, these OLED devices exhibited clear EL emission phenomena, that was the occurrence of the distinct light-emitting diode response characters at ca 5V. As increasing the driving voltages from ca. 5V, the device current of those OLEDs was sharply increased, along with the exponentially enhanced luminance from 0.01 to 0.1 cd m−2 to ca.10–50 cd m−2. Most importantly, as shown in Fig. 9b, the OLEDs based on R/G/B– BP/BODIPY exhibited perfect red, green, and blue EL emission with the emission peaks at 672, 515, and 435 nm, and the CIE coordinates of (0.35, 0.29), (0.34, 0.58) and (0.23, 0.25) respectively. Those EL spectra matched well with their corresponding PL spectra (Fig. S5), indicating that the EL emission from those OLEDs purely originated from these R/G/B-BP/BODIPY EMLs. Under EL operations, holes and electrons were individually injected, transported, and then recombined within those BP-capped BODIPY chromophores, which were followed by intrinsic excitons decays via EL emissions. During these processes, it was anticipated that BP moieties did not impede charge hopping transports for exciton generations, while they were additionally beneficial for enhancing EL emissions via its particular shielding effects. In addition, we noticed that such EL emissions were stable under different driving voltages (Fig. S6), which indicates that such solid-state EL emission is robust enough.

By contrast, we mentioned that those pure BODIPY samples cannot realize such EL spectra, which was due to the intense ACQ issues of pure BODIPY samples in solid-state. In this respect, it highlighted the peculiar contributions of BP moieties on the achievements of EL emissions of non-doped R/G/B-BP/BODIPY OLEDs. It was noticed that the device performance of those BP/BODIPY-based OLEDs was still moderate. For instance, the best device fabricated with G-BP/BODIPY as the EML showed a maximum current efficiency (CE) of 0.63 cd A−1 and a maximum luminance of 48.68 cd m−2. Although the performance of OLEDs is not adequate and further efficiency enhancement is expected through advanced device optimizations, the first successful integration of the 2D BP luminescent materials in OLEDs opens the door for the further design and development of BP-based composite light-emitting materials and devices with high mobility and high luminous efficiency.

In summary, we developed a facile strategy to synthesize chemically anchored BP/chromophore composite luminescent materials by simply sonicating BP nanosheets and BODIPY in acetonitrile solution. Three colors of red, green, and blue solid-state fluorescent emission were realized with more than 5 orders of magnitude increase of fluorescence intensity compared with the pure solids of BODIPY and BP. SEM, TEM, and LSCM images all indicated that BODIPY were dispersed on the surface of the BP nanosheets remaining in the 2D layered structure, which prevented the ACQ issue of BODIPY and induced the solid-state emission. The interaction mechanism has been investigated by FTIR, Raman, and XPS spectral, confirming the covalent interaction between BP and BODIPY, which passivated the surface of BP and eliminated oxidation. The first successful achievement of three single-colored OLEDs based on the R/G/B-BP/BODIPY composite luminescent materials provided a brand-new approach to further design and development of high mobility and high luminous efficiency composite luminescent materials and devices based on BP.

Yun Zhao: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Junyu Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Huilong Xu: Software, Investigation. Xijie Wang: Investigation. Bolin Zhao: Investigation. Baohua Zhang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhonghai Ni: Writing – review & editing. Guoling Li: Software, Investigation. Yuwei Zhang: Writing – review & editing.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2023QN1010), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22122402), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (no. 2021B1515020048).

ce:section.ce:para:
